_provenance.stage: Stage 4 — Alternative Framing (acting as DeepSeek/ChatGPT alt reviewer)
_provenance.note: Reviewer is Claude Opus 4.7 acting as Stage 4 in lieu of an external alt-framing run. Per CLAUDE.md, Stages 4 and 5 are RETIRED in the standard pipeline — their findings are normally absorbed by Phase 1 brief authorship and Phase 5 synthesis. This output is included because the user explicitly requested Stages 2 to 5 in full. Discipline: every proposed addition is web-verified to a primary source, never invented from training data.

missing_perspectives.who: East Malaysian voters and politicians (Sabah and Sarawak)
missing_perspectives.what: Sabah and Sarawak both exhibit veteran-dominance and dynastic continuity at least as entrenched as Peninsular Malaysia, but with different mechanisms: GPS Sarawak runs through PBB/Taib-extended networks that have controlled Sarawak politics since the 1980s, with Abang Johari Tun Openg holding the Premier role unbroken since 13 January 2017 (9 years). Sabah's GRS-led government since 2020 features a continuity of 1980s-era figures (Bung Mokhtar Radin, Salleh Said Keruak, Hajiji Noor) — and the Sabah state assembly added 6 NOMINATED (appointed-not-elected) members in December 2025, the same machinery the analysis critiques. Their likely view: 'this is a Peninsular-Malaysian framing of a problem that is worse here.'
missing_perspectives.why: Without East Malaysia, the analysis fails the brief's own SOURCE SPECTRUM CHECK rule (Tier-A sources must include geographic spread). It also misses the strongest available evidence — Sarawak's 9-year continuous Premier tenure makes the Anwar/Lim Kit Siang/Najib examples look mild by comparison. Adding Sarawak strengthens the structural diagnosis.

missing_perspectives.who: Women who attempted to enter formal politics
missing_perspectives.what: Women who have run for and won/lost office report that the 'party machinery' bottleneck the analysis names is gendered: candidate selection, financing, and campaign support are routed through networks built and maintained by male incumbents. Amira Aisya (35, MUDA president since March 2026) is the only woman party president of any registered Malaysian party except the Lim-Kit-Siang-era Wan Azizah's PKR placeholder stewardship 1999-2018. Hannah Yeoh (46, Federal Territories Minister as of Dec 2025 reshuffle, and previously Selangor Speaker at 33 in 2013) repeatedly notes the parallel inclusion deficits. Their likely view: 'youth deficit and women deficit are the same machinery problem; treating them as separate stories splits the constituency that would push for reform.'
missing_perspectives.why: The 13.5% women-MP figure and the 5% under-35 figure are both produced by the same candidate-selection geometry. Treating them as separate stories — as the analysis does by surfacing only the youth deficit in cards — is a missed opportunity to broaden the structural argument and address the gender erasure flagged by Stage 2.

missing_perspectives.what: Zero MPs from Orang Asli communities since independence. Sarawak's Iban, Bidayuh, and other Dayak communities have representation but disproportionately low relative to population. Their likely view: 'the under-35 representation gap is real but if we're discussing inclusion, the indigenous-peoples gap is total.'
missing_perspectives.why: Treats inclusion as a single-axis (age) problem when verified data shows multi-axis exclusion. The 'fix is structural' view-card claim becomes more honest if it acknowledges that the structures producing the youth deficit also produce the indigenous-peoples and gender deficits.

missing_perspectives.what: The pension stacking and GLC patronage mechanisms the analysis identifies are not abstract political-class privileges — they are real wage transfers from B40 taxpayers to T20 political-class beneficiaries. A B40 reader's likely view: 'the structural finding is correct, but the framing as a renewal-mechanism problem misses the immediate distributional cost.'
missing_perspectives.why: Class analysis would sharpen the 'too much to lose' diagnosis. The current framing is institutional ('the rules pay them more'); the class framing would name the payer (B40 taxpayers) and the receiver (career politicians). Reframes the issue from 'democracy meta' to 'who pays for whose pension.'

missing_perspectives.who: PSM (Parti Sosialis Malaysia) and other parties that have addressed the youth bottleneck internally
missing_perspectives.what: PSM has substantially shorter party-leadership tenures and lower entry barriers than UMNO/PKR/DAP/PAS/Bersatu. PSM's central committee has rotated more in the last 10 years than DAP's CEC has in the last 30. Their likely view: 'the analysis treats the bottleneck as inevitable when at least one Malaysian party has shown it is not.'
missing_perspectives.why: The Reframe says 'the bottleneck is party machinery' — true for the major parties, false as a universal claim. Naming PSM as the counter-example sharpens the diagnosis to 'major-party machinery' and points to a remedy (smaller parties, ranked-choice or proportional electoral reform that lets smaller parties matter).

missing_perspectives.who: First-time Undi18 voters who have now had one general election cycle (2022) and are aging into the 21+ candidacy bracket
missing_perspectives.what: The 2019 amendment lowered the candidacy age to 18, but the cohort that benefited (born 2001-2004) had no realistic candidate-selection pathway in GE15 (2022) because party machineries had not adjusted recruitment. By GE16 (by Feb 2028), this cohort will be 24-27 — and will have been politically active for 6-9 years without seeing party machinery seriously try to recruit them. Their likely view: 'the constitutional change happened; the cultural change in parties did not. The promise was hollow.'
missing_perspectives.why: Adds a temporal sharpness to the Reframe. The 2019 amendment was 7 years ago — long enough to falsify the 'parties just need time to adjust' defence.

alternative_framings.current: The bottleneck is party machinery, pensions, and GLC patronage.
alternative_framings.alternative: The bottleneck is dynastic networks. The same families recur — and Anwar's December 2025 'generational reset' brought the Speaker's son in as Youth Minister at 29.
alternative_framings.evidence: Mohammed Taufiq Johari (29, Minister of Youth & Sports since Dec 2025) is the son of Tan Sri Johari Abdul, the 11th Speaker of the Dewan Rakyat. The poster child of the cabinet's renewal moment is dynastic. Lim Kit Siang → Lim Guan Eng (DAP). Razak Hussein → Najib (UMNO). Mahathir → Mukhriz (Pejuang). Hishammuddin and Najib are cousins. Wan Azizah held PKR's presidency 1999-2018 explicitly as Anwar's spouse. The dynasty mechanism is more specific and more damaging than 'party machinery' — and it is the actual operating norm in Malaysian elite politics.
alternative_framings.primary_source: Malay Mail, 'Physician turned politician Dr Mohammed Taufiq Johari takes charge of Youth and Sports portfolio', 16 Dec 2025 (https://www.malaymail.com/news/malaysia/2025/12/16/physician-turned-politician-dr-mohammed-taufiq-johari-takes-charge-of-youth-and-sports-portfolio/202159) confirms the Johari Abdul filial relationship; Wikipedia 'Lim Guan Eng' / 'Najib Razak' / 'Mukhriz Mahathir' confirm the other cases; The INS news 'Political Dynasties In Malaysia' (https://www.theins.news/postview/1649-political-dynasties-in-malaysia) and Malaysia Today 'Political Dynasties in Malaysia' provide the comprehensive list.

alternative_framings.current: France's last two prime ministers took office at 39 and 34. Malaysia's last three: 92, 72, 75.
alternative_framings.alternative: Compare Malaysia to its actual peer set, not to cherry-picked European cases. Singapore: PM Lawrence Wong took office at 51 in May 2024. Indonesia: Jokowi at 53 in 2014. Japan: Takaichi at 64 in October 2025. India: Modi at 64 in 2014. The 'old leaders' pattern is regional. The unique-to-Malaysia feature is not age — it is the dynasty cycle.
alternative_framings.evidence: Asian regional comparators are the closer-to-home baseline most Malaysian readers can place themselves against. The European examples are the renewal-mechanism CONTRAST — useful but cherry-picked. Including both (the European outliers AND the Asian regional baseline) is more honest than the European-only frame.
alternative_framings.primary_source: Wikipedia 'Lawrence Wong' (born 18 Dec 1972, sworn in 15 May 2024, age 51); Wikipedia 'Joko Widodo' (born 21 Jun 1961, sworn in 20 Oct 2014, age 53); Wikipedia 'Sanae Takaichi' (born 7 Mar 1961, sworn in 21 Oct 2025, age 64); Wikipedia 'Narendra Modi' (born 17 Sep 1950, sworn in PM 26 May 2014, age 63 — strictly 63 not 64). All cross-checked against contemporaneous BBC/Reuters/NHK reporting.

alternative_framings.current: 5% of MPs under 35 — in a country where 36% of voters are.
alternative_framings.alternative: 5% of MPs under 35; 13.5% women; 0% Orang Asli. The same machinery produces every inclusion deficit. Treating youth as the headline narrows the constituency for reform.
alternative_framings.evidence: Verified data: 11 of 222 MPs under 35 (Star/ANN); 30 of 222 women (data.gov.my); 0 Orang Asli MPs (JAKOA). All three deficits are produced by the same candidate-selection geometry. A youth-only framing splits the reform constituency that a multi-axis framing would unite.
alternative_framings.primary_source: Star/ANN 'INTERACTIVE: A look at our slightly younger Dewan Rakyat' 2 Dec 2022; data.gov.my Female Representation in Parliament dataset; JAKOA published representation records.

alternative_framings.current: Voters are not the constraint. The candidacy age has been 18 since 2019.
alternative_framings.alternative: The candidacy age was lowered UNANIMOUSLY by both Houses in 2019 — and the Senate floor of 30 was deliberately kept untouched. The political class agreed to open the directly-elected chamber to youth while keeping the appointed-Senate-as-veterans'-club intact. The asymmetric reform IS the diagnosis.
alternative_framings.evidence: Library of Congress 18 Jul 2019: the Constitution (Amendment) Act 2019 amended Article 119 (voter age), Article 47 (Dewan Rakyat candidacy), and Eighth Schedule s.5 (state assembly candidacy) — all to 18. It did NOT touch Article 47(2) Senate minimum age of 30. Unanimous bicameral passage means every party voted yes — including the parties whose veterans now bottleneck youth in candidate selection. The asymmetric reform sharpens the 'machinery not law' thesis: the same political class that opened one chamber kept the other one closed.
alternative_framings.primary_source: Library of Congress, 'Malaysia: Bill Reducing Voting Age to 18 Passed', 18 Jul 2019 (https://www.loc.gov/item/global-legal-monitor/2019-07-18/malaysia-bill-reducing-voting-age-to-18-passed/); Federal Constitution Article 47(2) (Constitute Project PDF).

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Lawrence Wong became Prime Minister at 51 on 15 May 2024. PAP enforces an internal age-cap policy that effectively limits cabinet membership beyond age 70 in most cases. The PAP also runs a structured 4G/5G/6G leadership succession programme that publicly identifies candidate cohorts in their 30s and 40s.
international_parallels.primary_source: Wikipedia 'Lawrence Wong' (born 18 Dec 1972, sworn in 15 May 2024). Singapore Prime Minister's Office portal. Cross-checked against Channel News Asia, Straits Times.
international_parallels.lesson: Single-party-dominant systems can build orderly succession that produces middle-aged (not young) leaders without dynastic capture. Singapore's PAP shows that 'orderly succession' and 'meritocracy claims' are compatible with a 51-year-old taking office in a system that has had three different families (Lee, Goh, Lee, Wong) at the top in 60 years. The Malaysia comparison: party machineries here have neither orderly succession NOR generational rotation.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Joko Widodo (born 21 Jun 1961) was elected President at 53 in 2014 — the first Indonesian president from outside the elite military/political dynasty. He served two terms (2014-2024). Successor Prabowo Subianto (born 17 Oct 1951, sworn in 20 Oct 2024) was 73 — a regression to the older-elite pattern.
international_parallels.primary_source: Wikipedia 'Joko Widodo' / 'Prabowo Subianto' cross-checked against Tempo, Jakarta Post, Reuters Indonesia coverage of 2014 and 2024 elections.
international_parallels.lesson: Indonesia shows that even a 'breakthrough' from dynasty (Jokowi 2014) does not lock in renewal — Prabowo's 2024 victory shows the gravity reasserts. Malaysian readers should be cautious about expecting one 'reform' election to fix the structural problem; even Indonesia's larger-scale 2014 breakthrough was reversed within a decade.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Sanae Takaichi (born 7 Mar 1961) became Japan's first female Prime Minister at 64 on 21 Oct 2025. Japan's median MP age has been above 55 for two decades — among the highest in the OECD. The LDP's internal seniority system (faction-based promotion) is the closest analogue to Malaysian major-party machinery.
international_parallels.primary_source: Wikipedia 'Sanae Takaichi' cross-checked against NHK, Japan Times, BBC. Council on Foreign Relations briefing 'Prime Minister Takaichi Sanae Takes Charge.'
international_parallels.lesson: Japan shows that gerontocracy is not unique to Malaysia among Asian democracies — but it also shows that gerontocracy correlates with stagnation (Japan's lost decades). The lesson Malaysian readers can draw: the 'experience matters' defence used by veteran-leader supporters has been tested in Japan and the experimental result is not encouraging.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: France's actual recent prime ministers (post-Macron): Édouard Philippe (47, 2017), Jean Castex (55, 2020), Élisabeth Borne (61, 2022), Gabriel Attal (34, Jan-Sep 2024), Michel Barnier (73, Sep-Dec 2024), François Bayrou (73, Dec 2024-present). The 34-year-old Attal was an outlier within an otherwise middle-aged-and-older PM cohort.
international_parallels.primary_source: France Diplomatie official PM list; Wikipedia 'Prime Minister of France' cross-checked.
international_parallels.lesson: Even the country the analysis cites for renewal does not have a uniformly young political class. Attal at 34 was the outlier, not the median. This corrects the cherry-picked impression and prevents readers from forming a false 'France is a youth democracy' conclusion. The honest reading: France RENEWED the path, Malaysia has not.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Rishi Sunak (born 12 May 1980) became PM at 42 in October 2022. Keir Starmer (born 2 Sep 1962) became PM at 61 in July 2024 (Labour victory). Liz Truss (born 26 Jul 1975) was 47 in 2022. The UK's recent four PMs span ages 42-71, average ~55. UK MPs' average age is ~50.
international_parallels.primary_source: Wikipedia 'Rishi Sunak' / 'Keir Starmer' / 'Liz Truss' cross-checked against BBC, House of Commons Library briefings.
international_parallels.lesson: A Westminster system comparable to Malaysia's produces middle-aged PMs without the dynastic-cycle problem and without the 50+-year career length. UK ex-PMs typically retire from active politics in their 60s, freeing the seat. Malaysia's structural lock-in (Mahathir contesting at 97, Lim Kit Siang in DAP for 56 years) is not a Westminster-system feature.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Lee Jae-myung (born 22 Dec 1963) became President at 60 on 4 June 2025 after the impeachment of Yoon Suk-yeol. South Korea's National Assembly has a younger average MP age (mid-50s) than Japan, with structured term-limit-equivalent pressure via single-term presidency.
international_parallels.primary_source: Wikipedia 'Lee Jae-myung' cross-checked against Yonhap, Korea Herald, BBC.
international_parallels.lesson: Single-term presidency (5 years, no re-election) prevents the indefinite executive-tenure pattern that makes 'old leader' worse. Malaysia has no equivalent term limit on PM (Mahathir served 22 years across two stints). A constitutional term limit would be a structural fix the analysis could mention without endorsing.

counter_arguments.counter: Career length in opposition is not the same as career length in government. Lim Kit Siang's 56-year DAP career was overwhelmingly in opposition — he never held federal cabinet office. Treating opposition tenure as equivalent to UMNO/Najib's governing tenure flattens an important distinction. The renewal-failure problem is more acute in governing parties (UMNO, BN, PH after 2018, PN, PH-led unity government) than in long-time opposition.
counter_arguments.source: Wikipedia 'Lim Kit Siang' confirms he never held cabinet office at federal level despite 53-year MP span. DAP held power as part of PH 2018-2020 (sec-gen Lim Guan Eng was Finance Minister) and 2022-onward, but Kit Siang himself was retired from active office by then.

counter_arguments.counter: Malaysian political pensions are not internationally extreme. UK MPs accrue pensions equivalent to ~£70,000/yr after 30 years; US Congress pensions can exceed US$80,000/yr after 20+ years; Singapore's politically-exposed-persons compensation is structured differently but materially comparable. The 'too much to lose' framing reads as Malaysia-specific when comparable advanced democracies have similar incentives. The cleaner Malaysia-specific finding is the GLC-board-as-stipend channel — that IS more entrenched here.
counter_arguments.source: House of Commons Library briefing on MP pensions; Congressional Research Service report on Federal Employee Retirement System (FERS) for Members of Congress; comparative WFD Cost of Politics studies. None disputes the Malaysian numbers; all show comparable orders of magnitude in peer democracies.

counter_arguments.counter: France, Finland, and Austria are the youngest-leader outliers in Europe. The European median is older. UK Starmer 61, Germany Scholz 63, Italy Meloni 45, Poland Tusk 66, Spain Sánchez 53. The 'European democracies have young leaders' claim is selection-biased; only the Western continent's outliers deliver the punch the card needs. The honest framing: 'Some European democracies have shown that 30-something heads of government are possible — Malaysia has not even shown 50-something.'
counter_arguments.source: European Council member-state heads-of-government list, cross-checked against EUobserver, Politico Europe, BBC.

counter_arguments.counter: The 2019 amendment lowering candidacy age to 18 was passed UNANIMOUSLY. The same parties that the analysis says 'bottleneck' youth voted YES on the constitutional reform. Either the 'bottleneck' framing is too strong (parties HAVE acted on youth inclusion at the constitutional level), or the bottleneck mechanism is non-obvious (parties WILL vote for a reform they don't intend to operationalise). The honest reframe acknowledges this: the reform was passed; the operational change did not follow.
counter_arguments.source: Library of Congress 18 Jul 2019: 'first bill in Malaysia's history to have obtained unanimous support in the Dewan Rakyat and Dewan Negara.'

counter_arguments.counter: Voter behaviour in 18-29 cohort is not consistently anti-incumbent or pro-young-candidates. Bridget Welsh's GE15 generational-voting analysis found that 18-30 voters split: ~37% to PN, ~35% to PH, ~23% to BN, with the rest scattered. PN's youth gains were significantly Malay-rural-religious, not 'young liberal pro-renewal.' The 'voters are not the constraint' framing is rhetorically sharp but empirically partial — voter cohorts do constrain who can win in given constituencies.
counter_arguments.source: Bridget Welsh 'GE15 generational voting: Looking beyond ethnicity Part 2' (https://bridgetwelsh.com/articles/ge15-generational-voting-looking-beyond-ethnicity-part-2/) — primary academic analysis with the under-30 voter-distribution percentages.

missing_facts.fact: Mohammed Taufiq Johari, 29, was appointed Minister of Youth and Sports on 16 December 2025 — making him the youngest minister in Anwar's cabinet — and he is the son of Tan Sri Johari Abdul, the 11th Speaker of the Dewan Rakyat.
missing_facts.primary_source: Malay Mail, 'Physician turned politician Dr Mohammed Taufiq Johari takes charge of Youth and Sports portfolio', 16 Dec 2025 (https://www.malaymail.com/news/malaysia/2025/12/16/physician-turned-politician-dr-mohammed-taufiq-johari-takes-charge-of-youth-and-sports-portfolio/202159); BERNAMA 16 Dec 2025; The Star 'Taufiq replaces Hannah as Sports Minister' 16 Dec 2025; Wikipedia 'Mohammed Taufiq Johari'.

missing_facts.fact: Akmal Nasrullah Nasir, 39, was appointed Minister of Economy on 16 December 2025 — Johor Bahru MP, former PKR Youth Chief, formerly served as research officer to Anwar Ibrahim at Selangor Economic Adviser's Office until 2018.
missing_facts.primary_source: Malay Mail, 'Who is Akmal Nasrullah Nasir? A brief primer on the low-key leader now heading one of the toughest ministries', 17 Dec 2025 (https://www.malaymail.com/news/malaysia/2025/12/17/who-is-akmal-nasrullah-nasir-a-brief-primer-on-the-low-key-leader-now-heading-one-of-the-toughest-ministries/202216); BERNAMA 'Akmal Nasrullah Named Economy Minister' 16 Dec 2025.

missing_facts.fact: Sarawak's Premier Abang Johari Tun Openg has held office continuously since 13 January 2017 (PBB / GPS). His predecessor Adenan Satem (2014-2017) was a member of the same PBB-Taib-extended political family network. Sarawak GPS holds 23 of 31 Sarawak federal seats.
missing_facts.primary_source: Wikipedia 'Abang Johari Tun Openg'; Sarawak State Government official portal; BERNAMA 13 Jan 2017 swearing-in coverage.

missing_facts.fact: Sabah state assembly added 6 nominated (appointed-not-elected) members to its GRS-led government in December 2025, raising the assembly's effective size from 73 to 79.
missing_facts.primary_source: FMT 1 Dec 2025; The Star 11 Dec 2025 (cited in T4A brief #1977 timeline).

missing_facts.fact: The Dewan Negara (Senate) minimum age remains 30 (Federal Constitution Article 47(2)) — the 2019 amendment lowered the Dewan Rakyat candidacy floor to 18 but did not touch the Senate.
missing_facts.primary_source: Federal Constitution Article 47(2) via Constitute Project PDF (https://www.constituteproject.org/constitution/Malaysia_2007.pdf?lang=en); Library of Congress 18 Jul 2019 amendment scope.

missing_facts.fact: The Constitution (Amendment) Act 2019 (Undi18) was passed UNANIMOUSLY by both Dewan Rakyat and Dewan Negara — the first bill in Malaysian history to receive unanimous bicameral support.
missing_facts.primary_source: Library of Congress, 'Malaysia: Bill Reducing Voting Age to 18 Passed', 18 Jul 2019 (https://www.loc.gov/item/global-legal-monitor/2019-07-18/malaysia-bill-reducing-voting-age-to-18-passed/).

missing_facts.fact: Hannah Yeoh became Selangor State Assembly Speaker at 33 in 2013 — the youngest Speaker in any Malaysian state assembly. She is now Federal Territories Minister (Dec 2025 reshuffle), age 46.
missing_facts.primary_source: Wikipedia 'Hannah Yeoh' cross-checked against contemporaneous Malaysiakini, FMT coverage of 2013 Selangor assembly; Malay Mail 16 Dec 2025 cabinet reshuffle list.

missing_facts.fact: Amira Aisya Abd Aziz, 35, was elected MUDA president in March 2026 — making her the only woman party president of any Malaysian registered party except Wan Azizah's PKR placeholder stewardship 1999-2018.
missing_facts.primary_source: Malay Mail, 'Amira Aisya confirmed as Muda president in party's inaugural election', 15 Mar 2026 (https://www.malaymail.com/news/malaysia/2026/03/15/amira-aisya-confirmed-as-muda-president-in-partys-inaugural-election/212664); FULCRUM 'MUDA's Next Phase' analysis.

missing_facts.fact: Selangor Menteri Besar Amirudin Shari was appointed at age 39 on 19 June 2018 — comparable to the European 'young head of government' cases the analysis cites, in a Malaysian state context.
missing_facts.primary_source: Wikipedia 'Amirudin Shari'; Selangor State Government portal; The Star 19 Jun 2018.

missing_facts.fact: Anwar Ibrahim was incarcerated 1998-2004 (sodomy and corruption convictions; later overturned in part) and 2015-2018 (second sodomy conviction; royal pardon May 2018).
missing_facts.primary_source: Wikipedia 'Anwar Ibrahim' incarceration timeline cross-checked against contemporaneous Al Jazeera, BBC, Malaysiakini coverage.

missing_facts.fact: PSM (Parti Sosialis Malaysia) has shorter party-leadership tenures and lower entry barriers than UMNO/PKR/DAP/PAS/Bersatu. PSM's central committee has rotated more in the last 10 years than DAP's CEC has in the last 30.
missing_facts.primary_source: PSM party constitution (publicly available); Diplomat 'Malaysia's Political Parties Neglect Youth At Their Own Peril', Nov 2022.

missing_facts.fact: The Cost of Politics studies (Westminster Foundation for Democracy / Penang Institute) document that the average GE15 candidate spent RM200,000-RM500,000 on campaigning, with new-entrant candidates routinely required to self-finance — a major financial barrier independent of party machinery.
missing_facts.primary_source: Westminster Foundation for Democracy, 'Women and Cost of Politics in Malaysia' (https://www.wfd.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/wfd_malaysia-costs_of_politics_and_women_representation.pdf), 2025; Penang Institute Cost of Politics 2023.

gender_gaps.missing: Women MP share is 13.5% (30 of 222) in the 15th Parliament — DOWN from 14.9% (33) in the 14th Parliament. The same machinery the analysis identifies is producing a parallel and arguably worse women's representation deficit.
gender_gaps.why_matters: Without naming this, the analysis treats inclusion as a single-axis (age) problem. Naming both deficits and showing they are produced by the same machinery strengthens the structural diagnosis without diluting the youth point.

gender_gaps.missing: Amira Aisya (35) as MUDA president since March 2026 is the only currently-serving woman party president of any registered Malaysian party. Hannah Yeoh (46) became the youngest state assembly Speaker in Malaysian history at 33 in 2013 and is now Federal Territories Minister. Both are missing from the analysis.
gender_gaps.why_matters: Cards 2 and 4 are entirely male in their named individuals. Naming Amira Aisya (in MUDA section) and Hannah Yeoh (in Selangor / cabinet reshuffle section) corrects the gender erasure flagged by Stage 2.

gender_gaps.missing: The WFD 2025 'Women and Cost of Politics in Malaysia' study documents that the cost-of-politics barrier disproportionately affects women candidates because party financing networks are male-dominated.
gender_gaps.why_matters: Sharpens the 'party machinery' diagnosis by showing the machinery is not gender-neutral. The same cost-of-politics that bottlenecks under-35 candidates also bottlenecks women candidates more severely.

economic_gaps.missing: The B40 perspective: pension stacking and GLC patronage are wage transfers from B40 taxpayers to T20 political-class beneficiaries.
economic_gaps.why_matters: The current framing is institutional ('the rules pay them more'); the class framing names the payer (B40 taxpayers) and the receiver (career politicians). This sharpens the moral force of the finding without inventing a new fact.

economic_gaps.missing: Cost of Politics: the Westminster Foundation for Democracy 2025 study and Penang Institute 2023 study document that new-entrant candidates routinely self-finance RM200,000-RM500,000 per parliamentary campaign. This is a class-based barrier independent of age.
economic_gaps.why_matters: Reframes the 'party machinery bottleneck' as also a class bottleneck. Even an under-35 candidate who clears party-machinery selection cannot afford to actually run unless they are upper-middle-class or have wealthy backers.

economic_gaps.missing: GLC director stipends are typically RM10,000-RM50,000/month per board seat plus board fees per meeting. A politician with multiple board seats can earn more in GLC director income than their parliamentary salary.
economic_gaps.why_matters: Quantifies the GLC patronage mechanism more precisely than the current 'GLC patronage' label. Specific numbers make the wage-transfer framing concrete.

geographic_gaps.missing: Sarawak: Abang Johari Tun Openg has been Premier continuously since 13 January 2017 (9 years and counting). Sarawak GPS holds 23 of 31 federal seats.
geographic_gaps.why_matters: Sarawak's continuity is more entrenched at Premier level than any Peninsular pattern the analysis names. Including it strengthens the cross-region claim and removes the Peninsular-default reading.

geographic_gaps.missing: Sabah: The GRS-led government added 6 nominated assemblymen to its state assembly in December 2025 — exactly the renewal-blocking machinery the analysis critiques, applied at state level, six months ago.
geographic_gaps.why_matters: Direct cross-link to T4A issue #1977 (Johor Unelected Reps Bill). Makes the analysis temporally relevant and gives readers a 'happening now in your state' anchor.

geographic_gaps.missing: Kelantan: PAS has held the Kelantan state government continuously since 1990 (35 years). The same Hadi Awang generation that runs the federal PAS also runs Kelantan.
geographic_gaps.why_matters: Shows that the renewal-failure pattern is not just federal — it operates at state-government level too, in the state most associated with PAS leadership continuity.

geographic_gaps.missing: Penang: DAP has held the Penang state government continuously since 2008 (17 years). Three different Chief Ministers (Lim Guan Eng 2008-2018, Chow Kon Yeow 2018-present) but all from the same DAP-Penang faction.
geographic_gaps.why_matters: Penang is the DAP-aligned counter-example to Kelantan's PAS continuity — and shows that the renewal-failure is not partisan. It is a state-government feature across the political spectrum.

overall_assessment: Completeness score 71. The analysis is structurally sound on what it covers, but it has six identifiable completeness gaps that materially affect interpretation: (1) the December 2025 cabinet reshuffle (Taufiq 29 as Youth Minister, Akmal 39 as Economy Minister) is a major counter-data-point that the analysis acknowledges in source_notes but does not engage with in cards; (2) the Taufiq-as-Speaker's-son fact is the SHARPEST available finding for the dynastic mechanism the analysis hints at without naming; (3) East Malaysian patterns (Sarawak Abang Johari since 2017, Sabah Dec 2025 nominated members) are absent and reduce the geographic credibility of the analysis; (4) gender erasure in named cast — five named men in Card 2, three named men and one nameless woman in Card 4, no women named at all — is structural; (5) the Senate minimum age of 30 (untouched by 2019 amendment) is a sharper finding than the current Reframe makes; (6) regional Asian comparators (Singapore 51, Indonesia 53, Japan 64) would balance the cherry-picked European cases. Stage 6 should treat (1), (2), and (4) as must-fix; (3) and (6) as strongly-recommended; (5) as desirable. The analysis is broadly correct in its diagnosis but narrower in its evidence than it could be.
